content:
# Matthew Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE, page following Matthew 1 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_002_page_001.jpg

1. Now the Yeshua having been born in Bethleem of the Ioudaia in [the] days of Herodes the king, behold, magoi from [the] east arrived into Hierosolyma,

2. saying, "Where is the [one] having been born king of the Ioudaioi? For we saw the star of him in the east and came to do-obeisance to him."

3. And having heard, Herodes the king was troubled, and all Hierosolyma with him.

4. And having gathered together all the chief-priests and scribes of the people, he was inquiring from them where the Christos is being born.

5. And they said to him, "In Bethleem of the Ioudaia, for thus it has been written through the prophet:

6. 'And you, Bethleem, land of Iouda, by no means are you least among the rulers of Iouda; for out of you will come forth a ruler, who will shepherd the people of me, the Israel.'"

7. Then Herodes, having secretly called the magoi, ascertained exactly from them the time of the appearing star.

8. And having sent them into Bethleem, he said, "Having gone, search exactly for the young-child; and when you find [him], report to me, so that I also, having come, may do-obeisance to him."

9. And they, having heard the king, went; and behold, the star which they saw in the east was going before them until, having come, it stood above where the young-child was.

10. And having seen the star, they rejoiced [with] exceedingly great joy.

11. And having come into the house, they saw the young-child with Mariam the mother of him, and having fallen [down], they did-obeisance to him; and having opened the treasures of them, they offered to him gifts: gold and frankincense and myrrh.

12. And having been warned in a dream not to return to Herodes, by another way they withdrew into the country of them.

13. And of them having withdrawn, behold, an angel of [the] Lord appears in a dream to the Ioseph, saying, "Having risen, take the young-child and the mother of him and flee into Egypt, and be there until I tell you; for Herodes is about to seek the young-child to destroy him."

14. And he, having risen, took the young-child and the mother of him by night and withdrew into Egypt.

15. And he was there until the end of Herodes, in order that might be fulfilled the [word] having been spoken by [the] Lord through the prophet, saying, "Out of Egypt I called the son of me."

16. Then Herodes, having seen that he was mocked by the magoi, was exceedingly angry, and having sent, he killed all the boys in Bethleem and in all the borders of it, from two-years-old and under, according to the time which he had ascertained exactly from the magoi.

17. Then was fulfilled the [word] having been spoken through Ieremias the prophet, saying,

18. "A voice in Rama was heard, weeping and much lamentation: Rachel weeping [for] the children of her, and she would not be comforted, because they are not."

19. And of Herodes having died, behold, an angel of [the] Lord appears in a dream to the Ioseph in Egypt,

20. saying, "Having risen, take the young-child and the mother of him and go into [the] land of Israel; for those seeking the life of the young-child have died."

21. And he, having risen, took the young-child and the mother of him and came into [the] land of Israel.

22. But having heard that Archelaos is reigning over the Ioudaia in place of Herodes the father of him, he feared to go there; and having been warned in a dream, he withdrew into the regions of the Galilaia.

23. And having come, he dwelt in a city called Nazaret, so that might be fulfilled the [word] having been spoken through the prophets, that "Nazoraios he will be called."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **μάγοι (v.1):** Transliterated as "magoi" — the Greek term does not mean "wise men" or "kings." The μάγοι were Persian priestly astrologers associated with Zoroastrianism. Any translation smoothing this to "wise men" obscures the specific cultural reference.

2. **προσκυνῆσαι (vv.2, 8, 11):** Rendered as "do-obeisance" throughout. The verb προσκυνέω means to bow down in reverence — it could indicate worship of a deity or political obeisance to a ruler. The ambiguity is preserved; the text does not specify which sense is intended in each instance.

3. **παιδίον (vv.8-21):** Consistently rendered "young-child" — this is a diminutive form (not βρέφος/infant or υἱός/son), indicating a toddler-age child. The magoi do not visit a newborn in a manger but a young child in a house (οἰκίαν, v.11).

4. **Ναζωραῖος (v.23):** "Nazoraios" — the derivation of this term is debated. Matthew says it was spoken "through the prophets" (plural), but no single OT text says this verbatim. It may be a wordplay on נֵצֶר (netzer, "branch," Isaiah 11:1) or a reference to Nazarite vows, or simply the demonym for Nazareth. The ambiguity is preserved by transliteration.

5. **Sinaiticus reading at v.18:** The Sinaiticus reads θρῆνος καὶ κλαυθμὸς καὶ ὀδυρμὸς πολύς ("lamentation and weeping and much mourning"). Some manuscripts omit θρῆνος ("lamentation"). The fuller Sinaiticus reading is preserved.

— EMET, AI Translator
